coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence properties of l-cysteine-derived carbon dots prepared in non-aqueous and aqueous solvents
coredata.dc:creator: Yoshinaga, Taishu
Akiu, Moeka
Iso, Yoshiki
Isobe, Tetsuhiko
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) can be produced from amino acids such as l-cysteine by an intermolecular dehydration reaction between NH2 and COOH groups, followed by carbonization. This dehydration reaction may be hindered in aqueous media because the amino acid molecules are surrounded by water; therefore, we used a non-aqueous reaction media to facilitate the dehydration reaction. In the present study, l-cysteine was heated at 230 °C for 30 min in a non-aqueous solvent, diphenyl ether, which has a high boiling point of 258 °C, to yield CDs-NA. For comparison, we also hydrothermally prepared CDs-A from l-cysteine dissolved in water, with the use of an autoclave and microwave heating system under the same temperature and time conditions. The maximum photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the water dispersion of CDs-NA was 1.5 times as high as that of the CDs-A. The absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of CDs-NA was 4.2%, which was 1.4 times as high as that of CDs-A, 3.0%. Characterization of the elemental compositions, particulate properties, and chemical bonding states confirmed that the non-aqueous solvent not only facilitated CD formation via the dehydration reaction but also contributed to the formation of sulfonic acid groups by oxidation of thiol groups. The rapid particle growth and formation of the additional functional groups might contribute to the increased PL intensity and absolute PLQY of CDs-NA. Through the optimization of the synthesis conditions in a non-aqueous solvent, the absolute PLQY of CDs-NA was increased from 4.2% to 10.2%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Carbon dots
l-cysteine
Intermolecular dehydration reaction
Non-aqueous reaction media

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of l-cysteine-derived CDs in non-aqueous and aqueous solvents
PL properties of l-cysteine-derived CDs synthesized in non-aqueous and aqueous solvents
Elemental compositions, particulate properties, and chemical bonding states of l-cysteine-derived CDs synthesized in non-aqueous and aqueous solvents
Optimization of synthesis conditions of l-cysteine-derived CDs prepared in a non-aqueous solvent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 3D projection of molecular structure of l-cysteine.

(a) Photograph (λex: 365 nm), (b) PLE and PL spectra, and (c) PL decay curves (λex: 365 nm, λem: 440 nm) of 100 mg L−1 water dispersions of CDs-NA and CDs-A prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1.

(a, b) PL spectra, and changes in (c) emission wavelength and (d) PL intensity with varying excitation wavelength of 100 mg L−1 water dispersions of CDs-NA and CDs-A prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1.

(a, b) TEM images of CDs prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1. (c, d) Particle size distributions measured from the TEM images. (a, c) CDs-NA and (b, d) CDs-A.
FT-IR spectra of powders of CDs-NA and CDs-A prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 in the region of 400–4000 cm−1.
C 1s and S 2p XPS spectra of powders of CDs-NA and CDs-A prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1.

PL spectra of the 100 mg L−1 water dispersions (4 mL) of CDs-NA and CDs-A after adding Pb2+ solutions (100 μL) with the different concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, and 10 mM) under 352 and 318 nm excitation, respectively.
Changes in the absolute PLQYs of the water dispersions of CDs-NA and CDs-A prepared at 230 °C and the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 with heating duration.
Proportions of C, H, N, S, and O of l-cysteine, CDs-NA, and CDs-A prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1, determined by elemental analysis.
Assignment of FT-IR absorption peaks of Fig. 5.

row.entry: Combination band of δas(NH3 +) and the hindered rotation of NH3 +

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ν = stretching; δ = deformation or bending; ω = wagging; as = asymmetric; s = symmetric.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence properties of l-cysteine-derived carbon dots prepared in non-aqueous and aqueous solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Keio University, 3-14-1 Hiyoshi, Kohoku-ku, Yokohama, 223-8522, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Keio University, 3-14-1 Hiyoshi, Kohoku-ku, Yokohama, 223-8522, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) can be produced from amino acids such as l-cysteine by an intermolecular dehydration reaction between NH2 and COOH groups, followed by carbonization. This dehydration reaction may be hindered in aqueous media because the amino acid molecules are surrounded by water; therefore, we used a non-aqueous reaction media to facilitate the dehydration reaction. In the present study, l-cysteine was heated at 230 °C for 30 min in a non-aqueous solvent, diphenyl ether, which has a high boiling point of 258 °C, to yield CDs-NA. For comparison, we also hydrothermally prepared CDs-A from l-cysteine dissolved in water, with the use of an autoclave and microwave heating system under the same temperature and time conditions. The maximum photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the water dispersion of CDs-NA was 1.5 times as high as that of the CDs-A. The absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of CDs-NA was 4.2%, which was 1.4 times as high as that of CDs-A, 3.0%. Characterization of the elemental compositions, particulate properties, and chemical bonding states confirmed that the non-aqueous solvent not only facilitated CD formation via the dehydration reaction but also contributed to the formation of sulfonic acid groups by oxidation of thiol groups. The rapid particle growth and formation of the additional functional groups might contribute to the increased PL intensity and absolute PLQY of CDs-NA. Through the optimization of the synthesis conditions in a non-aqueous solvent, the absolute PLQY of CDs-NA was increased from 4.2% to 10.2%.

CD formation via intermolecular dehydration reactions between l-cysteine molecules and the formation of sulfonic acid groups were facilitated in a non-aqueous solvent than in an aqueous solvent, leading to enhanced PL properties of CDs-NA.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis strategies for fluorescent carbon dots have been explored for more than a decade; however, a need remains for more effective preparative methods. Xu et al. reported fluorescent carbon nanoparticles in 2004 in the process of the electrophoretic purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes prepared by arc discharge [1]. Sun et al. successfully produced fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser ablation of a graphite target in argon gas containing water vapor, followed by surface passivation with polyethylene glycol; they named these nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) [2]. CDs have since received considerable attention owing to their low toxicity, visible light emission, high photostability, and a characteristic property of excitation-dependent emission [2–13]. CDs have been rapidly fabricated by laser irradiation of graphite powders dispersed in organic solvents [9]. Zhou et al. pioneered electrochemical reactions of multiwalled carbon nanotubes to prepare CDs [10]. These CDs possessed hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxy groups, which have found applications in bioimaging [11–13]. However, the fabrication of CDs by breaking down carbon materials, carbon nanotubes, and graphite requires complex equipment, extended synthesis routes, and difficult purification steps [14–18].

Hydrothermal syntheses of CDs in an autoclave have been widely performed because this represents an eco-friendly and simple synthesis [19–21]. An aqueous solution of citric acid and ethylenediamine has been hydrothermally treated at 150–300 °C for 5 h to yield CDs [19]. An aqueous solution of sodium citrate and ammonium hydrogen carbonate was hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 4 h to prepare CDs [20]. CDs can be readily prepared by a hydrothermal reaction in a closed reactor fitted with a microwave heating system, in which heating is more uniform and the reaction time is shorter than a conventional electric heating system [22–24]. CDs have been prepared from an aqueous glucose solution through a hydrothermal treatment at 280–700 W for 1–11 min with the use of a microwave heating system [23]. Hydrothermally synthesized CDs have been shown to have applications in bioimaging [19,25], pH sensing [21], thermal sensing [21,26], detection of heavy metals [27–29], and color patterning [19,30].

Numerous works concerning the formation of CDs via an intermolecular dehydration reaction through hydrothermal treatment have been reported [31–35]. CDs were hydrothermally prepared via a dehydration reaction between NH2 and COOH groups of l-cysteine molecules, followed by carbonization [35]. We have investigated the optimal conditions for the synthesis of CDs through hydrothermal treatment of l-cysteine using a microwave heating system [36]. l-cysteine contains nitrogen and sulfur, as illustrated in Fig. 1 and S1. These elements likely contribute to the formation of new energy levels from heteroatom dopants, resulting in enhanced blue emission [37,38]. We verified that the promotion of the dehydration reaction between l-cysteine molecules is a key factor affecting the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the CDs [36]. On this basis, the dehydration reaction may be inhibited in hydrothermal synthesis because starting materials are surrounded by water. Moreover, water generated by the dehydration reaction remains in an autoclave, which is likely to inhibit further dehydration reactions; therefore, we have focused on non-aqueous systems without the use of an autoclave (a closed reactor) to promote the intermolecular dehydration reaction.

In the present study, we focused on diphenyl ether possessing a high boiling point of 258 °C as a non-aqueous solvent to prepare CDs in an open system; the generated water can be removed from system. l-cysteine was heated at 230 °C in a non-aqueous solvent of diphenyl ether with the use of an oil bath to prepare CDs-NA. For comparison, CDs-A were hydrothermally prepared from l-cysteine in an autoclave with the use of a microwave heating system at the same temperature. To understand the factors determining the differences in the PL properties between CDs-NA and CDs-A, we investigated their elemental compositions, particulate properties, and chemical bonding states.

l-cysteine (>98.0%, FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical), diphenyl ether (>99.0%, FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical), hexane (>95.0%, Kanto Chemical), and Pb(CH3COO)2·3H2O (>99.9%, Kanto Chemical) were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of l-cysteine-derived CDs in non-aqueous and aqueous solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method of CDs in a non-aqueous solvent is as follows. l-cysteine (0.65 g) was added into diphenyl ether (40 mL) to adjust an l-cysteine concentration to 133 mmol L−1. This mixture was heated at 230 °C for 30 min with the use of an oil bath whose maximum operating temperature was 250 °C under ambient conditions and without a reflux unit. After adding hexane (280 mL) into the obtained suspension, this mixture was centrifuged at ~19,000×g (13,000 rpm using a rotor with a diameter of 10 cm) for 20 min. The supernatant solution was removed to collect the precipitate. After adding hexane (280 mL) into the collected precipitate, the resulting mixture was centrifuged at the same rotation speed. The same procedure was repeated to yield a precipitate. After drying naturally for 1 day, the obtained powder was dispersed in ultrapure water (40 mL). The prepared dispersion was passed through a membrane filter (pore size 0.2 μm) to yield an aqueous dispersion of CDs-NA. A CDs-NA powder was obtained by freeze-drying the water dispersion for 24 h. The concentration of the water dispersion of CDs-NA was adjusted to be 100 mg L−1 for analysis.

The preparation of CDs in an aqueous solvent is described below [36]. l-cysteine (0.65 g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (40 mL) to adjust an l-cysteine concentration to 133 mmol L−1. The solution was purged with Ar gas for 30 min at a flow rate of 300 mL min−1 then transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) vessel (Berghof, DAK-70). The sealed PTFE vessel was heated at 230 °C for 30 min in an autoclave with a microwave heating system (Berghof, Speedwave MWS-2), which had a maximum operating temperature of 230 °C. After cooling the PTFE vessel to room temperature by placing it in water, the prepared suspension was centrifuged at ~16,000×g (12,000 rpm, rotor diameter of 10 cm) for 15 min to remove large particles of by-products. The obtained supernatant solution was passed through a membrane filter (pore size 0.2 μm) to yield an aqueous dispersion of CDs-A. CDs-A powder was obtained by freeze-drying the water dispersion for 24 h. The concentration of the water dispersion of CDs-A was adjusted to be 100 mg L−1 for analysis.
To optimize the PL properties, alternative synthesis conditions were also investigated for CDs-NA and CDs-A.

Photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and PL spectra of the CD dispersions were measured with a fluorescent spectrometer (JASCO, FP-6500). The spectral response was calibrated against a solution of rhodamine B in ethylene glycol (5.5 g L−1) and a standard light source (JASCO, ESC-333). PL decay curves of the dispersions were recorded on a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Hamamatsu Photonics, Quantaurus-Tau C11367), which was equipped with a 365 nm LED as the light source. The absolute PLQYs of the dispersions under 365 nm UV excitation were measured using a quantum efficiency measurement system (Otsuka Electronics, QE-2000-311C). Elemental compositions of the CD powders were determined with an elemental analyzer (Elementar Analytical, Vario EL). The particle sizes and morphologies of the CDs were observed with a field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Tecnai 12). The samples for TEM observation were prepared by drying a drop of each CD dispersion on a copper grid covered with a carbon-reinforced collodion film (Oken Shoji, COL-C10). UV–vis absorption spectra of the CD dispersions were measured with an UV–visible optical absorption spectrometer (JASCO, V-570). The net absorbance of the CDs was calculated by subtracting the absorbance of ultrapure water from that of the CD dispersion. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) absorption spectra of powder samples in pressed KBr disks were measured on a spectrometer (JASCO, FT/IR-4200). Chemical bonding states of the CD powders were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with an XPS instrument (JEOL, JPS-9010TR) using an Mg Kα radiation source. A portion of the powder surface was coated with Au by an ion sputtering device (Hitachi, E−1030) for charge-up correction.

To investigate thiol groups on the surface of CDs, the PL spectra of CDs-NA and CDs-A water dispersions in the presence of Pb2+ ions were measured under 352 and 318 nm excitation, respectively. Different amounts of Pb(CH3COO)2·3H2O (2, 10, 20, 40, 100, and 200 mg) were dissolved in ultrapure water (50 mL). These Pb2+ solutions (100 μL) with the different concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, and 10 mM) were added into 100 mg L−1 water dispersions (4 mL) of CDs-NA and CDs-A, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL properties of l-cysteine-derived CDs synthesized in non-aqueous and aqueous solvents
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 a, the 100 mg L−1 water dispersions of CDs-NA and CDs-A prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 exhibited blue emission under 365 nm near-UV excitation. The emission of the CDs-NA dispersion was clearly stronger than that of the CDs-A dispersion. Fig. 2b shows the PLE and PL spectra of these dispersions. The excitation and emission peaks of the CDs-NA dispersion were located at 352 and 432 nm, respectively, which were red-shifted compared with those of the CDs-A dispersion at 318 and 392 nm. These peaks are attributed to transitions between π-levels and surface state levels and between π-levels and nitrogen-doped levels [36].

We also measured PL decay curves of the same dispersions, as shown in Fig. 2c. These curves were fitted with the following tri-exponential equation (1):
(1)
f
( x )
=
A 1
exp
( − x /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
( − x /
τ 2
)
+
A 3
exp
( − x /
τ 3
)
where f (x) is the PL intensity at time x, A 1, A 2, and A 3 are constants, τ 1, τ 2, and τ 3 are decay times. The mean PL lifetime <τ> was calculated by the following equation (2):
(2)
< τ > =
τ 1 2
A 1
+
τ 2 2
A 2
+
τ 3 2
A 3
τ 1
A 1
+
τ 2
A 2
+
τ 3
A 3
Table S1 shows the PL lifetimes and fitting parameters calculated by fitting the PL decay curves. The mean PL lifetimes of the CDs-NA and CDs-A dispersions were 4.9 and 4.2 ns, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 470 nm, the emission peaks of the CDs-NA and CDs-A dispersions shifted from 413 to 533 nm and from 391 to 516 nm, respectively. The relationships between the emission and excitation wavelengths were slightly different for both dispersions, as shown in Fig. 3c. These excitation-dependent behaviors are attributed to different energy levels related to a variety of surface states and nitrogen-doped states [37]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the maximum PL intensity of the CDs-NA dispersion was 1.5 times as high as that of the CDs-A dispersion. The absolute PLQY of the CDs-NA dispersion was 4.2%, which was 1.4 times as high as that of the CDs-A dispersion, 3.0%. The higher PLQY is related to the longer mean PL lifetime. To understand the factors determining the difference in the PL properties between CDs-NA and CDs-A, we investigated their elemental compositions, particulate properties, and chemical bonding states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elemental compositions, particulate properties, and chemical bonding states of l-cysteine-derived CDs synthesized in non-aqueous and aqueous solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 shows the proportions of C, H, N, S, and O in CDs-NA and CDs-A powders prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1, as determined by elemental analysis. The proportions of H and O in the CDs-NA powder were lower than those in the CDs-A powder, whereas the proportions of C, N, and S in the CDs-NA powder were higher than those in the CDs-A powder. This result confirms that the dehydration reaction between l-cysteine molecules was more effectively facilitated in the non-aqueous solvent than that in the aqueous solvent.

Fig. 4 a and b shows the TEM images of CDs-NA and CDs-A prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1, respectively. Nanoparticles were observed for both samples. Fig. 4c and d shows the size distributions calculated from these images. The mean particle sizes of the CDs-NA and CDs-A samples were 11.5 ± 3.5 nm and 6.0 ± 1.6 nm, respectively. The rapid growth of the CDs-NA particles may be attributed to the promotion of the dehydration reaction in the non-aqueous solvent [35].

Fig. S2 shows UV–vis absorption spectra of the same dispersions, for which PL spectra and PL decays are shown in Figs. 2 and 3. In the spectrum of the CDs-A dispersion, a strong absorption (peak 1) and shoulder (peak 2) were observed at ca. 230 and 280 nm, respectively, which are assigned to the π→π* transition of CC bonds and the n→π* transition of CO bonds, respectively [23,39]. The absorbances of these peaks were higher for the CDs-A than those of CDs-NA. The broad absorption (peak 3) in the near-UV region is attributed to the transition from π-levels to surface state levels and nitrogen-doped levels [36,40,41]. The absorbance of peak 3 of the CDs-NA dispersion was slightly higher than that of the CDs-A dispersion. The peaks 1–3 were not observed in the spectrum of the l-cysteine aqueous solution, as also shown in Fig. S2.

Fig. 5 shows the FT-IR spectra of CDs-NA and CDs-A powders prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 and Table 2 shows the details of the peak assignments. The broad peak 1 at 3200–3700 cm−1 was observed in the spectra of both samples and is assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H and amide N–H bonds [42]. However, as shown in Fig. S3 and Table S2, this peak was not observed in the spectrum of l-cysteine, which does not have an amide N–H bond. This result indicates that the intermolecular dehydration reaction between carboxy and amino groups in l-cysteine progressed for both samples, resulting in the formation of the amide bonds. Peak 6 was observed for l-cysteine and CDs-A but not for CDs-NA, and is assigned to the combination band of the asymmetric deformation and the hindered rotation of NH3 + groups (protonated NH2 groups) [42]. Thus, the dehydration reaction for CDs-NA was promoted and resulted in the conversion of NH2 to NH in amide groups. Peak 9 at ca. 1450 cm−1 was observed in the spectra of both CDs-NA and CDs-A and is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–N bonds formed by the dehydration reaction [34]. This result indicates that the dehydration reaction progressed to form amide bonds in both samples. Peak 11 at 1172 cm−1 is attributed to the absorption of hydrates of sulfonic acid groups, i.e., –SO3 − H3O+ [42]. Peak 13 at 1042 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of SO bonds [42]. Peaks 11 and 13 were observed for CDs-NA but not for CDs-A. Hence, sulfur in the thiol groups of CDs-NA was oxidized to form the sulfonic acid groups [45,46]. Sulfonic acid groups readily react with water to form their hydrates [42], and the water may be formed through an intermolecular dehydration reaction.

Fig. S4 shows the FT-IR spectra of diphenyl ether and Table S3 shows the details of the respective peak assignments. The characteristic sharp peaks 2 and 3 at 1586 cm−1 and 1487 cm−1 were observed in the spectrum of diphenyl ether and are assigned to the carbon–carbon vibrations of two monosubstituted benzene rings [42]. Such peaks were not observed in the spectrum of CDs-NA, as shown in Fig. 5. This confirms that diphenyl ether was not involved in the formation of CDs.

Fig. S5 shows survey scan XPS spectra of the CDs-NA and CDs-A powders prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1. Fig. 6 shows high resolution scans of the C 1s region. The shapes of the spectra were noticeably different between both samples. The C 1s spectra were fitted by six peaks at 288.5, 288.0, 287.4, 286.1, 285.5, and 284.5 eV which correspond to C–NH x , CO, C–S, C–O, C–C, and CC bonds, respectively [23,35]. Table S4 shows the proportions of these bonds calculated from the areas of the fitted C 1s XPS peaks. The proportion of C–NH x for CDs-NA was lower than that for CDs-A. This result indicates that the dehydration reaction for CDs-NA was promoted to a greater extent in the non-aqueous solvent, which decreased the proportion of NH2 groups and is consistent with the FT-IR results. The proportion of C–S bonds in CDs-NA was higher than that in the CDs-A. This result might be attributed to the formation of volatile sulfur compounds through pyrolysis of l-cysteine in the aqueous solvent in an autoclave for CDs-A [47]. In contrast, such pyrolysis did not occur in the non-aqueous solvent system used to synthesize the CDs-NA. In fact, we qualitatively detected volatile sulfur compounds from the sulfurous smell after the preparation of CDs-A, whereas there was no such odor in the CDs-NA samples.

Fig. 6 also shows the S 2p XPS spectra, which were fitted by two peaks at 165.8 eV and 164.0, and assigned to –SO3H and –SH groups, respectively [48,49]. The presence of the former is consistent with the FT-IR results. As shown in Table S5, the proportion of sulfonic acid groups in CDs-NA was remarkably higher than that in CDs-A.

Fig. S5 shows the O 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of CDs-NA and CDs-A powders prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1. The respective O 1s spectra were fitted by two peaks at 533.4 and 532.0 eV, which are assigned to C–OH and CO bonds, respectively [50,51]. The respective N 1s spectra were fitted by two peaks at 402.0 and 400.5 eV, which correspond to N–C and N–H bonds, respectively [52].

To confirm the differences of the surface functional groups between the CDs-NA and CDs-A, we investigated the potential for detection of Pb2+ ions, which preferentially bind thiol groups on the surface of CDs based on the HSAB theory [53]. Fig. 7 shows the PL spectra of the 100 mg L−1 water dispersions of the CDs-NA and CDs-A in the presence of Pb2+ ions. The PL intensity of the CDs-NA dispersions remained constant at all concentrations. Conversely, the PL intensity of the CDs-A dispersions gradually decreased as the concentration of Pb2+ ions was increased from 0.1 to 10 mM. These results confirm that the displacement reaction occurred between thiol groups on the surface of the CDs-A and Pb2+ ions, because the soft base of RS− was more effectively bound to the soft acid of Pb2+ than to the hard acid of H+ according to the HSAB theory [53,54]. This reaction triggered the electron transfer from CDs-A to Pb2+ ions, resulting in PL quenching [54].

The above-mentioned results confirmed that the dehydration reaction for CDs-NA was promoted more effectively than that for CDs-A. This resulted in the rapid particle growth and the formation of CD surface and doped nitrogen which created energy levels contributing to stronger emission and higher PLQY of CDs-NA [23,35,37]. In particular, sulfonic acid groups of CDs-NA might serve as surface state levels for enhancing PL properties. The red-shifted excitation and emission peaks of the CDs-NA (Figs. 2b and 3d) may reflect the difference in surface state levels between CDs-NA and CDs-A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions of l-cysteine-derived CDs prepared in a non-aqueous solvent
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S6 shows the FT-IR spectra of CDs-NA powders prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 without and with a reflux unit and Table S6 shows the details of the peak assignments. When a reflux unit was used, a weak FT-IR absorption peak 6 at ca. 2060 cm−1 was observed in the spectrum of CDs-NA; however, this peak was not observed when the reflux unit was not used. Peak 6 is assigned to the combination band of an asymmetric deformation and the hindered rotation of NH3 + groups in l-cysteine [42]. This result indicates that the dehydration reaction between l-cysteine molecules was promoted when the CDs-NA sample was prepared without a reflux unit, because water formed through the dehydration reaction was removed from the system. In addition, the absolute PLQY values of the water dispersions of CDs-NA prepared at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 under ambient and argon atmospheres were 4.2% and 3.6%, respectively. The ambient atmosphere might contribute to the formation of sulfonic acid groups by oxidation of thiol groups, leading to the improved PLQY.

On the basis of the above-mentioned results, we prepared CDs-NA in an open system using a flask without a reflux unit for a reaction time 30 min. We then used a reflux unit to prevent diphenyl ether from volatilizing during heating.

Fig. 8 shows the change in the absolute PLQY of the water dispersions of CDs-NA and CDs-A with heating duration. The absolute PLQY values of the CDs-NA dispersions gradually increased from 3.6% to 7.1% as the heating duration increased from 15 to 240 min. Conversely, the absolute PLQY of the CDs-A dispersions saturated at 3.1% as the duration was increased to 120 min. Thus, the maximum PLQY of the CDs-NA dispersions was higher than that of the CDs-A dispersions.

CDs-NA was also prepared at 250 °C for 240 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1. The absolute PLQY of the CDs-NA dispersion prepared at 250 °C was 8.9%, which was higher than that prepared at 230 °C, 7.1%. Hence, a higher heating temperature improved the absolute PLQY. Moreover, when the l-cysteine concentration was increased from 133 mmol L−1 to 266 mmol L−1 and 399 mmol L−1, the absolute PLQYs of the CDs-NA dispersions were increased from 8.9% to 10.2% and 10.1%, respectively. Longer heating duration, higher temperature, and a higher l-cysteine concentration facilitated the formation of CD surface and doped nitrogen through a smooth dehydration reaction. This resulted in the increased PLQY value of CDs-NA [23,35–37]. On the other hand, suppression of the dehydration reaction in an aqueous solvent might lead to the saturated PLQY value. The upper limit for the reaction was 250 °C, which is close to the boiling point of diphenyl ether at 258 °C [55]. Further investigations on the synthesis conditions in a non-aqueous other solvent with a higher boiling point might be expected to further improve PL properties of CDs-NA.

CDs-NA were prepared from l-cysteine in a non-aqueous solvent of diphenyl ether at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 in an open system using a flask. For comparison, a CDs-A sample was synthesized from l-cysteine in an aqueous solvent via a hydrothermal reaction at 230 °C for 30 min at the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 in an autoclave with a microwave heating system. The maximum PL intensity of the water dispersion of CDs-NA was 1.5 times as high as that of CDs-A. The absolute PLQY of the CDs-NA dispersion was 4.2%, which was 1.4 times as high as that of the CDs-A dispersion, 3.0%. Elemental analysis indicated that the proportions of H and O in CDs-NA were lower than those in CDs-A; however, the proportions of C, N, and S in the CDs-NA were higher than those in CDs-A. TEM observations indicated that the mean particle sizes of the CDs-NA and CDs-A samples were 11.5 ± 3.5 nm and 6.0 ± 1.6 nm, respectively. These results suggest that dehydration reactions between l-cysteine molecules were promoted to a greater extent in a non-aqueous solvent than in an aqueous solvent, contributing to the rapid particle growth. FT-IR and XPS spectra verified that a portion of the thiol groups in the CDs-NA sample was oxidized to form sulfonic acid groups. The rapid particle growth and the formation of these groups might contribute to the improved PL properties of CDs-NA. As the heating duration at 230 °C and the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1 was increased from 15 to 240 min, the absolute PLQY of the CDs-NA dispersion gradually increased from 3.6% to 7.1%. When the heating temperature was increased from 230 °C to 250 °C with heating for 240 min and the l-cysteine concentration of 133 mmol L−1, the absolute PLQY of the CDs-NA dispersion was increased from 7.1% to 8.9%. When the l-cysteine concentration was increased from 133 mmol L−1 to 266 mmol L−1 with heating at 250 °C for 240 min, the absolute PLQY of the CDs-NA dispersion was increased from 8.9% to 10.2%. Further investigations on the synthesis conditions in a non-aqueous other solvent with a boiling point higher than 260 °C might be expected to further improve the PL properties of CDs-NA. The enhancement of PL properties of CDs obtained from l-cysteine in a non-aqueous reaction media may be an applicable phenomenon for CDs prepared from other precursors like amino acids, because the non-aqueous system may facilitate the intermolecular dehydration reaction between NH2 and COOH groups of any kinds of precursors, leading to the rapid particle growth and the improved PL properties of the resulting CDs.
Taishu Yoshinaga: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation. Moeka Akiu: Visualization, Investigation. Yoshiki Iso: Supervision. Tetsuhiko Isobe: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2020.117260.
